title: Dog Walking with Legs Crossing Over (Scissoring Gait): Causes and Triage
meta_desc: Is your dog walking with legs crossing over? Learn about scissoring gaits, when to see a vet, and why recording a video helps with a diagnosis.
content_html:
TL;DR: A scissoring gait in dogs indicates a neurological lack of coordination and typically requires a veterinary evaluation within 24 to 48 hours, unless accompanied by sudden paralysis or severe pain.

What does it mean if my dog is crossing their legs or has a scissoring gait?

When a dog walks with their legs crossing over each other, it is often referred to professionally as a scissoring gait or ataxia. This movement is a sign of lack of coordination and typically indicates that the dog is unaware of where their paws are in space, a condition known as proprioceptive deficit. It usually stems from an underlying issue within the neurological system, such as a problem with the spinal cord, brain, or inner ear.

Is it a medical emergency if my dog's legs cross while walking?

  • The urgency level for a dog crossing their legs while walking is categorized as Medium.
  • While this is a serious clinical sign, it is generally not an immediate life-threatening emergency unless the dog is also experiencing sudden paralysis, severe pain, or a complete inability to stand.
  • If you notice this gait, you should schedule an appointment with your veterinarian or a veterinary neurologist within the next 24 to 48 hours to prevent further progression.

How does taking a video of my dog's gait help the veterinarian?

  • Taking a video of your dog walking is one of the most effective ways to help your veterinary team.
  • Gait abnormalities can sometimes be subtle or intermittent, and your dog may act differently due to the adrenaline of being at the clinic.
  • A clear video allows the veterinarian to observe the scissoring movement in a relaxed home environment, helping them pinpoint whether the issue is originating from the neck, mid-back, or brain more accurately.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Scissoring gait, where the legs cross over each other during walking, can be associated with several neurological conditions. Lesions affecting the L6 to S2 nerve roots as they exit the spinal cord can cause atrophy of the gluteal, semimembranosus, semitendinosus, and all muscles below the stifle. Arterial thromboembolism secondary to myocardial disease or other underlying disorders like hyperadrenocorticism, hypothyroidism, renal disease, cancer, and heart disease can also lead to pelvic limb ischemia, resulting in paraparesis and gait abnormalities. A crossed extensor reflex may be present with lesions above L6 (UMN sign).

Chapter: Neurology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1260)

faqs.question: What are common causes for a dog crossing their legs?
faqs.answer: Common causes include Intervertebral Disc Disease (IVDD), degenerative myelopathy, spinal tumors, or vestibular disease.

faqs.question: Should I let my dog exercise if they are crossing their legs?
faqs.answer: No, you should strictly limit your dog's activity and use a leash for bathroom breaks until they have been evaluated by a professional.

faqs.question: Can joint pain cause this walking pattern?
faqs.answer: While arthritis or hip dysplasia can cause weakness, crossing the legs is more typically associated with neurological dysfunction rather than simple joint pain.
